mappings.rationaleAI: Bacon's empirical inductive method directly addresses the radical reformulation of natural knowledge (A.4.b.i), the 17th-century debate about scientific methodology (B.2.b), and empirical procedures of science (B.3.a, B.3.b).

Empiricism's development from Locke through Hume and Kant's synthesis traces the 17th-18th century epistemological debates (B.2.b, B.2.c), while its influence on logical positivism and the Vienna Circle connects to 20th-century philosophy of science (B.2.e).

Scepticism's influence on scientific methodology from Pyrrhonian suspension to Hume's radical scepticism bears on the philosophy of science's development (B.2.b) and the validation of scientific theories (B.4.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 1
mappings.rationaleAI: The Royal Society's founding in the 1660s embodies the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b.i), Baconian empiricism, and the establishment of scientific societies (A.4.b.ii).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 2
mappings.rationaleAI: The Royal Society under Newton's presidency and its 18th-century role address characteristics of European science (A.4.c) and intellectual origins of the Enlightenment (A.5.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 4
mappings.rationaleAI: The Royal Society's 20th-century role in government-funded research and science policy addresses the social organization of science (A.7) and contemporary problems facing science (A.8, B.6).

The emergence of the professional scientist from natural philosopher traces the 19th-century professionalization (A.6), 20th-century social organization (A.7), and science's social significance (B.6).

Vitalism's relationship to Romantic Naturphilosophie (A.5.d) and its eventual replacement by mechanistic biology represent 19th-century progress in biology (A.6) and illustrate changing philosophical positions toward science (B.2.d).

Renaissance mathematics covers the state of science in the early 15th century (A.4.a.i), the influence of technology on science (A.4.a.ii), and the coexistence of new discoveries and old philosophical views (A.4.a.iii).

The history of optics from Greek theories through Islamic contributions to the Scientific Revolution illustrates science in Greek civilization (A.2.a), Islamic science (A.3), and the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b).

The British Enlightenment's empiricist tradition from Locke to Hume and Newton's scientific legacy address the spirit of the Enlightenment (A.5.b) and Empiricist interpretations of Newtonian physics (B.2.c).

Galen's integration of Hippocratic medicine with Aristotelian philosophy illustrates science in Greek civilization (A.2.a), his authority persisting through medieval science (A.2.c), and his influence on Islamic medicine (A.3).
Al-Biruni's empirical approach to natural philosophy and his pioneering methods in astronomy, mathematics, and geography exemplify Islamic science (A.3).
The early history of electricity from Franklin through Faraday traces science during the Industrial Revolution (A.5.a) and 19th-century progress in physics (A.6).
Newton's Laws of Motion and the Principia embody the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b) and shaped Empiricist, Rationalist, and Kantian interpretations of Newtonian physics (B.2.c).
Romanticism's reaction against Enlightenment rationalism connects directly to the Romantic reaction and its relationship to science, including Naturphilosophie (A.5.d).

Renaissance astrology illustrates the coexistence of new scientific discoveries and old philosophical views (A.4.a.iii), showing the gradual separation of astrology from science during the Scientific Revolution.

The nature of mathematics and its unreasonable effectiveness in natural sciences bears on the formal structures of science (B.3.c) and the relationship between philosophical analysis and scientific practice (B.5.b).

Popper's falsifiability criterion, critique of logical positivism, and critical rationalism are central to 20th-century philosophy of science (B.2.e), validation of theories (B.4.b), and the status of scientific propositions (B.5.a).
Social Darwinism illustrates the misapplication of scientific concepts to social policy (B.6) and 19th-century interactions between biology and social thought (A.6).

Alchemy's history from Hellenistic Egypt through Islamic civilization to early modern Europe illustrates medieval science (A.2.c), Islamic science (A.3), and the coexistence of old and new philosophical views (A.4.a.iii).

The 12th-century Renaissance's recovery of classical texts through Arabic translations and the founding of universities connect to medieval science (A.2.c) and Islamic contributions to Western science (A.3).

The music of the spheres from Pythagoras through Kepler illustrates the deep connection between mathematics and science in Greek civilization (A.2.a) and the transition from ancient to modern scientific cosmology.
The idea of progress in Western thought, from Enlightenment optimism through 20th-century critiques, connects to the spirit of the Enlightenment (A.5.b) and the social significance of science (B.6).
